metadata.title: The Number That Makes Galaxy-Spanning Computers Look Weak
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = scale building / astronomical numbers; CRIMSON = intuition failing / 'that's big' breaking down
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no SHA-256 internals, no collision-resistance details, no Bitcoin ASIC. Wonder register.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: To crack SHA-256 by brute force, you'd need a supercomputer spanning four billion Milky Ways, running for 37 times the age of the universe — and you'd still probably fail.

card.copy: The Number That Makes Galaxy-Spanning Computers Look Weak

beats.narration_text: 2^256 sounds like a big number. But "big" doesn't capture it. Let's build it from things we can see.

production_viz.label: 2^256 scale-building setup — single GPU icon with hash rate label

beats.narration_text: One GPU runs about 10^9 guesses per second. Pack 4 billion of them together: 4×10^18 per second. Better — but 2^256 is roughly 10^77. That's still 10^59 times too slow. Even 4 billion such machines isn't close.

card.sub: Building from one GPU to galaxy-scale compute

beats.narration_text: Scale again. Give one of those 4-billion-GPU machines to every person on Earth — 8 billion people, so 3.2×10^28 guesses per second. Now multiply by 4 billion copies of Earth — 1.3×10^38 per second. Still nowhere near.

production_viz.label: Stack: 4B GPUs per person × 8B people × 4B Earth copies — guesses/sec tally

beats.narration_text: Now replicate Earth 4 billion times per galaxy, and replicate the galaxy 4 billion times: roughly 2^160 guesses per second. Run this for 37 universe lifetimes — about 2^96 seconds. Total guesses: roughly 2^256. And your probability of success is only about 25%.

production_viz.label: Galaxy × galaxy copies × 37 universe lifetimes → 2^256 total guesses; 25% success probability label

beats.narration_text: Each "4 billion" step adds 32 bits to what you can search. 2^256 requires 8 such steps stacked. The security is not in any one hard step — it is in the cumulative multiplicative stack.

production_viz.label: 8 layers each labeled +32 bits stacking to 256 bits total; TEAL bars

beats.narration_text: This is why cryptographers say 256-bit security is "computationally infeasible." Not "very hard." Not "takes a long time." Physically impossible with any number of atoms in the observable universe running for any number of universe lifetimes.

production_viz.label: Text panel: 'computationally infeasible' vs 'very hard' — CRIMSON strike-through on naive labels

beats.narration_text: By contrast, 40-bit security — once common in export-grade crypto — is searchable on a laptop in minutes. 56-bit DES was cracked in 22 hours by a custom machine in 1999. 128-bit is already beyond physical attack. 256-bit has enormous margin.

production_viz.label: Key length comparison bar: 40-bit (laptop), 56-bit DES (22 hrs), 128-bit (safe), 256-bit (galaxy-spanning); CRIMSON for cracked, TEAL for safe

beats.narration_text: Why do we use 256 bits if 128 is already uncrackable? Defense in depth. Quantum computers with Grover's algorithm would halve the effective key length — making 256-bit security effectively 128-bit against a quantum attacker. Still safe, with margin.

production_viz.label: Grover halving diagram: 256-bit → 128-bit effective under quantum attack; safety margin arrow

beats.narration_text: 2^256 equals 4 billion to the power of 8. One GPU per person on 4 billion galaxy copies, running 37 universe lifetimes, 25% chance. The number is real. It is just incomprehensibly large.
med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
